skill.name: design-an-interface
skill.description: Generate multiple radically different interface designs for a module using parallel sub-agents. Use when user wants to design an API, explore interface options, compare module shapes, or mentions "design it twice".
skill.summary: Apply the 'Design It Twice' methodology to generate and compare radically different interface designs using parallel sub-agents.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: After evaluation, all 29 static findings are false positives. The SKILL.md file is a legitimate design methodology guide based on 'A Philosophy of Software Design.' The 'external_commands' detection at line 28 is a documentation example showing a prompt template in a code block, not actual command execution. The 28 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are erroneous pattern matches—this file contains no cryptographic code whatsoever. The skill instructs users to spawn parallel sub-agents to generate interface designs, which is a standard AI workflow pattern. No malicious intent, no data exfiltration, no unsafe operations detected.

content.user_title: Design Better Interfaces Using Parallel Sub-Agents
content.value_statement: Designers often settle on the first interface idea without exploring alternatives. This skill applies the 'Design It Twice' methodology, using parallel sub-agents to generate radically different interface designs for comparison and synthesis.
actual_capabilities: Guides gathering of module requirements before design begins
Spawns 3+ parallel sub-agents to generate radically different interface designs
Assigns distinct constraints to each sub-agent to enforce variation
Presents designs sequentially with interface signatures, usage examples, and hidden complexity
Compares designs on simplicity, generality, efficiency, depth, and ease of use
Synthesizes insights from multiple designs into a final recommendation
limitations: Focuses on interface shape only—does not implement the module
Requires the Task tool to spawn parallel sub-agents
Best suited for design exploration, not production-ready code generation
Output quality depends on the clarity of initial requirements

use_cases.title: API Design Exploration
use_cases.description: Explore multiple API shapes for a new library or service before committing to one.

use_cases.title: Module Architecture Decisions
use_cases.description: Compare radically different module interfaces to find the best abstraction for a complex problem.

use_cases.title: Teaching Interface Design
use_cases.description: Demonstrate the 'Design It Twice' methodology to teams learning software design principles.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Interface Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an interface for a user authentication module. Requirements: support login, logout, password reset, and session validation. Generate 3 radically different designs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new module and want to explore options

prompt_templates.title: API Redesign Exploration
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an existing pagination API: getPage(pageNumber, pageSize). Redesign it with 3 radically different approaches—one minimizing method count, one maximizing flexibility, one optimized for the common case.

prompt_templates.title: Comparative Design Constraints
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an interface for a file storage module. Spawn sub-agents with these constraints: Agent 1 minimizes methods (1-3 max), Agent 2 maximizes flexibility, Agent 3 optimizes for streaming large files. Compare the results.

prompt_templates.title: Synthesis Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I've generated 3 interface designs for a caching module. Compare them on simplicity, generality, and depth. Then synthesize a final design that combines the best elements.

output_examples.input: Design an interface for a retry mechanism with 3 radically different approaches.
output: Design 1 (Minimal): A single retry(fn, options) function with 3 options (maxAttempts, delay, backoff)
Design 2 (Flexible): A RetryPolicy class with configurable strategies (exponential, linear, custom) and a retry executor
Design 3 (Common-case): Specialized methods: retryWithBackoff(), retryUntil(), retryOnce()—each optimized for a specific pattern
Comparison: Design 1 is simplest but less flexible. Design 2 handles edge cases well. Design 3 is easiest to use correctly for common cases.

output_examples.input: Compare these two interface designs for a logging module.
output: Design A: logger.info(msg), logger.warn(msg), logger.error(msg)—flat structure, 3 methods
Design B: logger.log(level, msg) with level enum—1 method, more verbose calls

Trade-off: Design A is easier to use (logger.error is clear), but Design B allows custom log levels without API changes. Depth analysis: Design A hides less complexity (thin module), Design B hides more (deeper module).

best_practices: Gather complete requirements before spawning sub-agents to ensure meaningful variation
Assign distinct constraints to each sub-agent to enforce radical difference in approaches
Present designs sequentially so the user can absorb each approach before comparison
Compare designs on depth, simplicity, and ease of correct use—not just implementation effort
anti_patterns: Do not let sub-agents produce similar designs—enforce radical difference through distinct constraints
Do not skip the comparison step—the value is in contrasting approaches, not collecting options
Do not implement the module—this skill is purely about interface shape exploration

faq.question: What is the 'Design It Twice' methodology?
faq.answer: It is a principle from 'A Philosophy of Software Design' that says your first interface idea is rarely the best. By designing multiple radically different options, you gain insight into trade-offs and often discover a better solution.

faq.question: How many sub-agents should I spawn?
faq.answer: Spawn at least 3 sub-agents to get meaningful variation. More agents (4-5) can help if the design space is large, but 3 is usually sufficient to surface radically different approaches.

faq.question: What constraints should I assign to each sub-agent?
faq.answer: Give each agent a different design philosophy: one minimizing method count, one maximizing flexibility, one optimizing for the common case, or one taking inspiration from a specific paradigm.

faq.question: Does this skill implement the module?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses on interface design only. After selecting or synthesizing a design, you would implement it separately.

faq.question: Can I use this for existing module redesigns?
faq.answer: Yes. Pass the current interface and its limitations as context, then ask sub-agents to generate radically different alternatives.

faq.question: How do I compare designs objectively?
faq.answer: Compare on interface simplicity, generality vs specialization, implementation efficiency, depth (small interface hiding complexity), and ease of correct use vs misuse.
